title: A Semi-Analytical Solution for Slug Test by Considering Near-Well Formation Damage and Nonlinear Flow

research.question: Can slug-test water-level recovery in a fractured confined aquifer be modeled analytically when near-well formation damage and non-Darcy flow both add head loss?
research.system: A fully penetrating finite-radius test well in an infinite homogeneous confined aquifer, with a thin wellbore skin and nonlinear Forchheimer flow across the screen or gravel pack.
research.inputData: Radial confined-flow equations, slug-test well mass balance, field normalized water-level data from five tests at wells LA-87B, LA-88B, and LA-88A in the Minnelusa fractured aquifer, and Greene et al. (1999) predictions.
research.method: The model couples a Robin-type skin loss and quadratic Forchheimer loss to radial aquifer flow and well storage, derives Laplace-domain solutions with successive integration by parts, numerically inverts them with Stehfest, and fits transmissivity, storativity, skin factor, and Forchheimer coefficient.
research.benchmark: The Laplace solution reduces to Cooper et al. (1967) when skin and non-Darcy effects vanish, is compared with a finite-element solution, and is evaluated against three nested field-fit cases plus Greene et al. (1999).
research.uncertainty: Model cases are compared with mean error and standard error of estimate; the paper reports an unexpected pressure ordering in one fitted non-Darcy coefficient and attributes it to possible test or measurement quality rather than a probabilistic uncertainty model.
research.conclusion: Case 3 gives the smallest reported errors at LA-87B and LA-88B, but at LA-87B formation damage improves fit only marginally and its fitted effect is minor, leaving stronger evidence for non-Darcy loss; both effects are small at LA-88A.

narrative.throughline: A slug pulse does not recover through the aquifer alone: linear skin resistance and nonlinear screen loss reshape the well response and therefore the inferred aquifer parameters.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: The audience understands that a slug test displaces water in a well and infers aquifer properties from the recovery curve.
narrative.stakes: Fitting a fractured-aquifer slug test with Darcy flow alone can mistake near-well head loss for aquifer transmissivity or storage.
narrative.resolution: The recovery curve is decomposed into aquifer diffusion, thin-skin loss, and rate-dependent Forchheimer loss, then tested against field curves with and without those mechanisms.

background.premise: A slug test estimates aquifer parameters by suddenly displacing well water and analyzing the measured recovery with a mathematical model.
background.visibleEvidence: A water column is displaced inside a well and relaxes toward its initial level while a radial head pulse enters the aquifer.
background.whyNow: The well water-level curve is the scientific object whose unexpected shape motivates the new model.
background.boundary: The paper treats a fully penetrating well in a confined aquifer rather than every slug-test geometry.
background.sourceExcerpt: suddenly displacing a small quantity of water inside the well,
background.visualObject: Confined-aquifer well with an initially displaced water column and radial recovery field
background.motionPurpose: Release the displaced column so the recovery curve and outward aquifer response appear together.
background.stepDetail: Mark equilibrium; impose the initial displacement; release the slug; trace the falling normalized well level.
background.transitionBridge: The recovery curve approaches the well screen where extra losses occur.

background.premise: Drilling-induced formation damage creates a disturbed near-well skin whose permeability change causes linear head loss.
background.visibleEvidence: A thin annulus around the screen darkens and adds a head drop proportional to screen flow.
background.whyNow: The first missing mechanism is a linear resistance localized outside the well screen.
background.boundary: The model represents the disturbed zone as an infinitesimally thin Robin-type skin rather than solving a finite-thickness damage zone.
background.sourceExcerpt: Formation damage may cause a linear head loss because of
background.visualObject: Thin low-permeability skin annulus around the well screen
background.motionPurpose: Add a proportional head discontinuity as flow crosses the damaged annulus.
background.stepDetail: Draw the undisturbed aquifer; add the skin annulus; pass radial flow through it; reveal the linear head drop.
background.transitionBridge: Inside the skin, flow accelerates through the screen and gravel pack.

background.premise: Rapid flow across a screen or gravel pack can violate Darcy's law and add a nonlinear rate-dependent head loss.
background.visibleEvidence: Flow arrows crowd at the screen and the head-loss bar curves upward with increasing flux.
background.whyNow: The late-time recovery shape cannot be assigned only to the linear skin when the near-screen gradient is large.
background.boundary: The nonlinear loss is represented with the quadratic Forchheimer relation near the wellbore.
background.sourceExcerpt: water flow across the well screen or gravel pack sometimes
background.visualObject: Screen-scale flux arrows beside a curved head-loss-versus-flux relation
background.motionPurpose: Increase screen flux until the loss departs visibly from a straight Darcy relation.
background.stepDetail: Plot linear loss; increase flux; bend the loss curve upward; attach the excess loss to the screen.
background.transitionBridge: The skin and nonlinear screen losses join the radial aquifer model.

methods.input: Radially symmetric confined aquifer with a fully penetrating slug-test well
methods.output: Transient aquifer-head equation around the well
methods.validity: Darcy's law and continuity apply in the homogeneous aquifer outside the localized near-well loss region.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.1 Model Setup, Equation 1
methods.sourceExcerpt: aquifer h(r, t) [L] for the radially symmetrical flow induced
methods.visualObject: Radial aquifer rings carrying a decaying head pulse
methods.motionPurpose: Convert the released slug into radial diffusion through the confined aquifer.
methods.stepDetail: Center the well; draw radial distance; spread the head pulse; attach transmissivity and storativity controls.
methods.transitionBridge: The innermost aquifer ring meets the skin boundary.

methods.input: Aquifer head immediately outside the well and water level inside the well
methods.output: Linear screen-head discontinuity controlled by skin factor
methods.validity: A Robin-type boundary represents a thin damaged zone with head loss proportional to flow rate.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.1 Model Setup, Equation 2
methods.sourceExcerpt: left-hand side of Equation 2 represents a linear head loss
methods.visualObject: Head profile stepping across a thin annular skin
methods.motionPurpose: Insert a proportional discontinuity between well level and aquifer head.
methods.stepDetail: Align well and aquifer heads; pass flux through the skin; offset the heads; label the proportional loss.
methods.transitionBridge: The same screen flux enters the well water-volume balance.

methods.input: Screen flux around the well circumference
methods.output: Rate of water-level change inside the test well
methods.validity: Conservation of mass equates total screen inflow with the changing water volume in the well casing.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.1 Model Setup, Equation 4
methods.sourceExcerpt: mass, the total flowrate across the well screen should equal
anchorTerms: water volume inside the test well
methods.visualObject: Screen inflow arrows filling the changing well-water column
methods.motionPurpose: Tie radial screen flux directly to the observed well-level recovery.
methods.stepDetail: Sum flux around the screen; convert it to casing-volume change; move the well level by the balanced amount.
methods.transitionBridge: The screen flux now receives the nonlinear Forchheimer resistance.

methods.input: Hydraulic gradient and flux across the well screen
methods.output: Combined linear and quadratic near-screen flow resistance
methods.validity: The Forchheimer equation represents transitional non-Darcy flow and reduces to Darcy's law when its nonlinear coefficient is zero.
methods.locator: PDF page 3, Section 2.1 Model Setup, Equations 5-7
methods.sourceExcerpt: Considering the effect of non-Darcy flow, the Forchheimer equation is adopted to delineate the relation between
methods.visualObject: Linear and quadratic resistance bars acting on one screen-flux arrow
methods.motionPurpose: Curve the flux-gradient relation so rate-dependent loss becomes a visible second contribution.
methods.stepDetail: Show the linear term; add the quadratic term; combine their resistance; solve for the allowed screen flux.
methods.transitionBridge: The coupled aquifer-well equations receive dimensionless scales.

methods.input: Dimensional coupled aquifer-head and well-level equations
methods.validity: Initial displacement, well radius, transmissivity, and storage provide the paper's characteristic head, length, and time scales.
methods.sourceExcerpt: To simply the expression, dimensionless parameters/variables are introduced as follows:
methods.visualObject: Physical well radius, time, and head collapsing to dimensionless axes
methods.motionPurpose: Reduce the parameterized geometry to reusable dimensionless response curves.
methods.stepDetail: Divide head by initial displacement; divide radius by well radius; scale time by aquifer diffusion; expose dimensionless skin and nonlinear groups.
methods.transitionBridge: The normalized nonlinear system folds into Laplace space.

methods.validity: Laplace transformation converts temporal derivatives and the initial slug displacement into an algebraic-transform boundary problem.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The Laplace transform method with the integration by parts formula
methods.visualObject: Time-domain recovery curve folding into a Laplace-domain response
methods.motionPurpose: Move the nonlinear time dependence into transform space where the radial boundary problem can be derived.
methods.stepDetail: Transform aquifer head; transform well level; carry initial conditions; expose the nonlinear transformed term.
methods.transitionBridge: The nonlinear transformed term is expanded by successive integration.

methods.input: Nonlinear power term in the Laplace-domain screen relation
methods.validity: Brady's successive integration-by-parts expressions approximate the Laplace transform of a nonlinear power function.
methods.locator: PDF page 2, Section 1 Introduction, final paragraph; PDF page 4, Section 2.2
methods.sourceExcerpt: Brady (1969) work, in which they summarized several expressions of
methods.visualObject: Nonlinear transform block unfolding into successive boundary and integral terms
methods.motionPurpose: Expose the intermediate terms that make the nonlinear transform analytically tractable.
methods.stepDetail: Isolate the power term; integrate by parts once; repeat successively; collect the retained transformed expression.
methods.transitionBridge: The resulting Laplace solution is ready for numerical inversion.

methods.validity: The Stehfest numerical inversion algorithm evaluates the paper's transformed solutions in physical time.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.2 Dimensionless Solutions, paragraph following Equation 12
methods.visualObject: Laplace-domain curve reassembling as normalized water level versus logarithmic time
methods.motionPurpose: Return the solution to the same recovery curve measured in the field.
methods.stepDetail: Choose inversion terms; evaluate transformed response; sum Stehfest weights; plot normalized well level through time.
methods.transitionBridge: The recovered time-domain response retains the zero-loss limit examined in M09.

methods.input: Equation 9 with formation-damage and non-Darcy losses set to zero and equal casing and effective-well radii
methods.output: The Cooper et al. 1967 Laplace-domain slug-test solution
methods.validity: The limiting comparison requires negligible skin and non-Darcy effects plus equality of the casing and effective-well radii.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.2 Dimensionless Solutions, paragraph following Equation 12
methods.sourceExcerpt: reduces to Cooper et al.’s (1967) solutions in the Laplace domain when the effects of both formation damage and
methods.visualObject: Present transformed response collapsing onto the Cooper zero-loss curve
methods.motionPurpose: Expose the classical solution recovered when both added loss mechanisms vanish.
methods.stepDetail: Set the skin and nonlinear-loss controls to zero, match the two radii, and overlay the limiting curves.
methods.transitionBridge: The verified limiting response is carried into an independent finite-element solve in M10.

methods.input: Dimensionless slug-test equations after the zero-loss check, with formation-damage and non-Darcy losses restored
methods.validity: Supporting Information Text S2 supplies the finite-element solution used for the independent numerical check.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.2 Dimensionless Solutions, final paragraph
methods.sourceExcerpt: given in Text S2 in the Supporting Information S1
methods.visualObject: Finite-element mesh producing an independent normalized well-level response
methods.motionPurpose: Generate the numerical response before any overlay with the semi-analytical curve.
methods.stepDetail: Apply the nonlinear equations on the finite-element domain, solve the discrete system, and retain the time-domain response.
methods.transitionBridge: The generated finite-element response joins the semi-analytical response for comparison in M11.

methods.input: Time-domain semi-analytical response and independently generated finite-element response
methods.output: Numerical verification of the semi-analytical solution
methods.validity: The paper compares the Stehfest-inverted response with the finite-element solution supplied in Supporting Information Text S2.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.2 Dimensionless Solutions, final paragraph
methods.visualObject: Semi-analytical and finite-element recovery curves lying on common axes
methods.motionPurpose: Overlay independent solutions so disagreement would appear as a visible gap.
methods.stepDetail: Plot the semi-analytical response, add the finite-element response, and inspect their separation across time.
methods.transitionBridge: The verified response moves onto measured Minnelusa well data in M12.

methods.input: Normalized Minnelusa well-level observations and Equation 9
methods.output: Transmissivity, storativity, skin factor, and Forchheimer coefficient estimates
methods.validity: Levenberg-Marquardt nonlinear fitting evaluates nested physical cases against the same measured water-level data.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 3.1 Field Data Analysis
methods.sourceExcerpt: built-in Mathematica routine NonLinearModelFit to analyze the data for determining
methods.visualObject: Model recovery curve approaching field points as four parameter controls settle
methods.motionPurpose: Make the inferred aquifer and near-well parameters visibly responsible for the fitted curve shape.
methods.stepDetail: Load normalized observations; initialize parameters; compute the model curve; update parameters to reduce residuals.
methods.transitionBridge: Three nested model curves remain for error comparison in M13.

methods.input: Fits that omit both losses, include skin only, or include both losses
methods.output: Site-specific ME and SEE rankings for LA-87B, LA-88B, and LA-88A
methods.validity: Case 3 has the smallest reported errors at LA-87B and LA-88B, but the three LA-88A predictions are close; at LA-87B, adding formation damage alone improves fit only marginally and its fitted effect is minor.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 3.1 Field Data Analysis, Table 1
methods.sourceExcerpt: the parameter estimation are considered. Case 1 neglects both Sf
methods.visualObject: Three site-labeled recovery-curve groups with scalar ME and SEE readouts
methods.motionPurpose: Keep each case ranking attached to the well whose observations produced it.
methods.stepDetail: Show the three curves and error scalars for each well, retain the Case 3 ranking at LA-87B and LA-88B, and keep the close LA-88A results together.
methods.transitionBridge: The site-qualified rankings return to the physical well and its separately supported loss mechanisms.

symbols.visualObject: Displaced water level inside the test well
symbols.meaning: Well water-level displacement during slug recovery
symbols.transition: The moving well-water line receives the label H(t).

symbols.meaning: Hydraulic head in the confined aquifer
symbols.transition: A radial head ring at distance r receives the label h(r,t).

symbols.visualObject: Thin damaged annulus around the screen
symbols.transition: The annular head-drop marker becomes the label S_f.

symbols.visualObject: Curvature of the nonlinear screen-loss relation
symbols.transition: The quadratic loss curvature receives the label beta.

symbols.visualObject: Radial spreading rate of the aquifer head pulse
symbols.transition: The radial diffusion control is labeled T.

symbols.visualObject: Aquifer storage released under the radial head pulse
symbols.transition: The storage fill beneath the head pulse is labeled S.

return.visualObject: Minnelusa fractured-aquifer well with the measured normalized recovery curve and the Case 1, Case 2, and Case 3 predictions tied back to skin and nonlinear screen loss
return.claim: For the tested wells, Case 3 gives the smallest reported errors at LA-87B and LA-88B, while the three cases remain close at LA-88A; at LA-87B, the evidence for non-Darcy loss is stronger because formation damage improves fit only marginally and its effect is minor.
return.claimBoundary: These are site-specific fits under the fully penetrating homogeneous radial model. LA-88A shows small or negligible loss effects, and one LA-88B pressure trend was inconsistent with expectation.
return.locator: PDF page 5, Section 3.1 Field Data Analysis; PDF page 8, Section 4 Conclusions
return.sourceExcerpt: formation damage in the present solution only marginally improves the model fit

visual.primaryObject: Fully penetrating slug-test well and radial confined-aquifer head field
visual.accentObject: Thin skin annulus and nonlinear screen-loss curve
visual.geometryNotes: Keep the well, screen, and radial aquifer rings persistent; add the skin as a thin annulus, map screen flux to the well-volume balance, curve the Forchheimer loss with rate, and overlay nested model responses on the measured logarithmic-time recovery curve.
